stem: The locus of points \( z = x+iy \) satisfying \( |z - (([[a1]])+([[a2]])i)| = [[r]] \) is a circle of radius \( [[r]] \) centered at \( ([[a1]], [[a2]]) \). Which of the following is a value of \( y \) for a point on this circle whose real part is \( x = [[x0]] \)?
solution: Substituting \( x=[[x0]] \) into \( (x-([[a1]]))^{2}+(y-([[a2]]))^{2}=([[r]])^{2} \) and solving for \( y \) gives two values; one of them is \( y = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the full radius to the center's \( y \)-coordinate, ignoring the horizontal offset \( x - a_1 \).
This subtracts the full radius from the center's \( y \)-coordinate, ignoring the horizontal offset.
This uses the horizontal offset directly as a shift in \( y \) without the Pythagorean relationship the circle equation requires.
This is off by one from a genuine solution.
This is off by one from a genuine solution.
This has the wrong sign from a genuine solution.

stem: The locus of points \( z = x+iy \) satisfying \( |z - (([[a1]])+([[a2]])i)| = |z - (([[b1]])+([[b2]])i)| \) is the perpendicular bisector of the segment joining \( ([[a1]], [[a2]]) \) and \( ([[b1]], [[b2]]) \). Find the \( y \)-coordinate of the point on this locus whose real part is \( x = [[x0]] \).
solution: Setting the two distances equal, \( (x-([[a1]]))^{2}+(y-([[a2]]))^{2} = (x-([[b1]]))^{2}+(y-([[b2]]))^{2} \), the \( y^{2} \) terms cancel, leaving a linear equation in \( y \). Substituting \( x=[[x0]] \) and solving gives \( y = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the midpoint's \( y \)-coordinate, which lies on the bisector only when \( x_0 \) equals the midpoint's \( x \)-coordinate, not in general.
This confuses the midpoint's \( x \)-coordinate for the requested \( y \)-coordinate.
This is the difference of the two centers' \( y \)-coordinates, not a point on the bisector.
This is off by one from a genuine solution.
This is off by one from a genuine solution.
This has the wrong sign from a genuine solution.

stem: The locus of points \( z = x+iy \) satisfying \( \arg\!\left(z - (([[a1]])+([[a2]])i)\right) = \dfrac{[[theta_num]]\pi}{4} \) is a ray starting at \( ([[a1]], [[a2]]) \). Find the \( y \)-coordinate of the point on this ray whose real part is \( x = [[x0]] \).
solution: The ray's slope is \( \tan\!\left(\dfrac{[[theta_num]]\pi}{4}\right) \). Setting \( \dfrac{y-([[a2]])}{[[x0]]-([[a1]])} \) equal to this slope and solving for \( y \) gives \( y = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This flips the sign of the slope, running the ray in the opposite direction.
This is just the center's \( y \)-coordinate, ignoring the ray's slope entirely.
This confuses the given \( x \)-coordinate for the requested \( y \)-coordinate.
This is off by one from a genuine solution.
This is off by one from a genuine solution.
This has the wrong sign from a genuine solution.

stem: The point \( w = ([[w1]]) + ([[w2]])i \) lies on the locus \( |z - (([[a1]])+([[a2]])i)| = r \), a circle centered at \( ([[a1]], [[a2]]) \). Find the radius \( r \).
solution: The radius is the distance from the center to the boundary point: \( r = |w-a| = \sqrt{(([[w1]])-([[a1]]))^{2}+(([[w2]])-([[a2]]))^{2}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the absolute values of the coordinate differences instead of using the Pythagorean combination.
This is \( r^{2} \), the square of the radius; the square root is still needed.
This is the modulus of \( w+a \), not the distance \( |w-a| \) between them.
This is off by one from the correct radius.
This is off by one from the correct radius.
This doubles the correct radius.
